title: Proving an Image Was Built by Your Pipeline
description:
## Tamper-Evident Provenance

After a high-profile supply-chain incident, leadership asks for a control that lets the platform **cryptographically prove a container image was built by your official CI pipeline and has not been tampered with since**, and to **refuse to run any image that fails that proof** at deploy time.

Which combination of mechanisms directly satisfies that requirement?

options.text: Have the pipeline generate signed build provenance / attestations (e.g. SLSA provenance) and sign the image with cosign, then enforce signature/attestation verification at the Kubernetes admission stage (e.g. a verifying admission controller / policy) so unsigned or unverifiable images are rejected.

Generate an SBOM for every image with Syft and scan it with Grype; if no critical CVEs are found, the image is proven authentic and safe to run, so block only images with critical vulnerabilities at admission.

Push images only to a private registry over TLS and require pull credentials; since only the pipeline can push and the transport is encrypted, the image's origin and integrity are guaranteed without any signing.

Pin every image by its `:latest`-replaced semantic version tag and record the tag in Git; because the tag is immutable once recorded, matching the deployed tag to Git proves the image is the one your pipeline built.

explanation: Proving origin and integrity is a cryptographic problem. The pipeline signs the image (cosign) and produces signed provenance/attestations — ideally SLSA provenance describing how, where, and from what the artifact was built. At deploy time an admission controller (e.g. a Sigstore policy controller / Kyverno / Connaisseur-style verifier) checks the signature and attestation against the trusted public key/identity and rejects anything it can't verify. That is what makes 'built by our pipeline, untampered' enforceable. (b) conflates two different concerns: an SBOM plus vulnerability scanning tells you what is inside and whether it has known CVEs, but it says nothing about who built the image or whether it was swapped/tampered — a clean scan does not prove authenticity. (c) is wrong: TLS protects the image in transit and registry auth limits who can push, but neither produces tamper-evidence after the fact, and a compromised pipeline/registry credential or a registry breach defeats it; there's no signature to verify origin. (d) is wrong: tags are mutable by default and even an 'immutable' tag is just a pointer — recording a tag in Git doesn't cryptographically bind the bytes; only a digest plus a signature does. (Pinning by digest is good practice, but it still isn't a proof of who built it.)

hints.title: Origin/integrity is cryptographic
hints.body: Proving 'we built it and nobody changed it' needs signatures and attestations, not scanning or transport security.

hints.title: SBOM answers a different question
hints.body: An SBOM lists components for vulnerability tracking. SLSA provenance + cosign signing answers 'who built this and is it untampered', and admission verification enforces it.
